mechanism: A child states a feeling using an 'I am [state]' construction. The dad deliberately mishears/reinterprets the feeling-word as if it were the child's given name, and responds with the culture's standard first-introduction greeting, introducing himself as 'Dad'. The comic engine is a universal category-confusion (mistaking a description for an identity), not a sound-based pun, so it is rebuilt natively in each language using that language's own idiomatic 'to be [state]' phrasing and its own self-introduction formula, keeping setup/reveal/effect aligned across all four languages.

en.combined: Dad, I'm tired. / Hi Tired, I'm Dad.
en.portability_notes: Uses 'to be' + adjective ('I'm tired'), the same grammatical shape used natively by French (être fatigué) and Spanish (estar cansado), so the mock-name substitution lands the same way in all three.

fr.combined: Papa, je suis fatigué. / Salut Fatigué, moi c'est Papa.
fr.portability_notes: French naturally uses 'être fatigué' (to be tired), matching the English adjective-after-copula shape, so 'Fatigué' substitutes for a name exactly as 'Tired' does in English. 'Moi c'est Papa' is the natural, idiomatic French self-introduction formula (more natural here than a literal 'je suis Papa').

es.combined: Papá, estoy cansado. / Hola Cansado, soy Papá.
es.portability_notes: Spanish uses 'estar cansado' (to be tired), the same predicate-adjective shape as English and French, so 'Cansado' works as a mock name the same way. 'Hola X, soy Papá' mirrors the natural Spanish self-introduction pattern.

ja.punchline: はじめまして疲れた、パパだよ。
ja.combined: パパ、疲れたよ。 / はじめまして疲れた、パパだよ。
ja.portability_notes: 疲れた (tsukareta, 'got tired'/'I'm tired') is a single predicate word, so it substitutes naturally for a name the same way the adjectives do in the other three languages. はじめまして is the standard phrase used specifically upon a first self-introduction, so treating '疲れた' as the just-introduced name and replying with はじめまして...だよ reproduces the same mock-introduction beat, not a bolted-on clause as in loop 01.

revision_reason: Replaced anchor feeling-word 'hungry' (avoir faim / tener hambre, a possession+noun construction in French/Spanish) with 'tired' (être fatigué / estar cansado, a to-be+adjective construction in French/Spanish, matching English 'I'm tired'), so the name-mock-introduction mechanism keeps the same grammatical shape and comic beat across all four languages, and reworked the Japanese line to read as a natural exchange rather than a clunky bolted-on clause.
